title: What Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
A Phase II archaeological evaluation is one of the most consequential steps in the federal cultural resource management (CRM) process. When a Phase I survey identifies potentially significant archaeological sites on or near a project area, agencies and developers must determine whether those sites are eligible for listing on the National Register of Historic Places — and that determination is the purpose of a Phase II archaeological evaluation.

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted investigation of sites identified during a Phase I survey to determine whether they meet National Register of Historic Places eligibility criteria. It involves controlled excavation, laboratory analysis, and a formal report used by federal agencies to guide Section 106 compliance decisions.

Understanding what a Phase II archaeological evaluation entails — and when one is required — is critical for federal agencies, state transportation departments, and private developers working on federally funded or permitted projects. Getting this phase right protects both the public interest in irreplaceable heritage resources and project timelines.

What Does a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Involve?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation involves controlled subsurface excavation and systematic data collection designed to answer a specific question: does this site possess the integrity and significance needed for National Register eligibility? The investigation goes far deeper — literally and analytically — than the reconnaissance-level work of a Phase I survey.

Fieldwork typically includes hand-excavated test units placed across the site according to a sampling strategy designed to expose deposits, features, and artifact distributions. Unit size, spacing, and depth are driven by the site's characteristics and the research questions outlined in a project-specific work plan approved by the relevant State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO).

Laboratory analysis follows fieldwork. Recovered artifacts are cleaned, catalogued, and analyzed by material type — ceramics, lithics, faunal remains, botanical samples, and more. Radiocarbon dates, soil profiles, and feature documentation contribute to an understanding of site age, function, and research potential. The final deliverable is a comprehensive technical report presenting findings, significance assessments, and an eligibility recommendation.

When Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Required?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation is required whenever a Phase I survey identifies an archaeological site that cannot be determined eligible or not eligible for the National Register without additional data. This threshold is triggered under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) when a federal undertaking — a project involving federal funding, permits, or land — has the potential to affect historic properties.

Not every site found in a Phase I survey proceeds to Phase II. Consulting parties, typically the lead federal agency and the SHPO, review Phase I findings and make a preliminary determination. Sites clearly lacking integrity, those previously disturbed beyond significance thresholds, or sites whose surface assemblages alone demonstrate ineligibility may be recommended as not eligible without further testing.

Sites that appear intact, contain features, or show evidence of research potential — particularly for periods or themes underrepresented in the regional record — are strong candidates for Phase II evaluation. State DOTs, the Army Corps of Engineers, FHWA, and other agencies routinely encounter this requirement during infrastructure and construction projects across the Southeast.

What Are the National Register Eligibility Criteria Being Tested?

The National Register of Historic Places evaluates properties under four criteria (A through D), and most archaeological sites are assessed primarily under Criterion D — whether the property has yielded, or may yield, information important to history or prehistory. A Phase II archaeological evaluation gathers the evidence needed to apply these criteria rigorously.

Criterion D eligibility hinges on two related questions: Does the site contain intact, interpretable deposits? And does the site have research potential to address significant archaeological or historical questions? A site can be rich in artifacts but still lack eligibility if disturbance has destroyed the contextual relationships that make those artifacts meaningful.

Evaluators examine vertical stratigraphy, feature density and diversity, artifact diversity and density, and whether the site can contribute to understanding regional patterns of settlement, subsistence, technology, or exchange. Bioarchaeological evidence, when present, can elevate significance under additional criteria related to history (Criterion A) or association with important persons (Criterion B), though Criterion D remains the primary standard for most archaeological sites in archaeological assessments.

How Long Does a Phase II Evaluation Take?

The timeline for a Phase II archaeological evaluation varies considerably based on site size, complexity, and laboratory demands, but most projects move from fieldwork through final report delivery in three to six months. Larger or more complex sites — particularly multicomponent sites with deep stratified deposits — can extend that timeline significantly.

Fieldwork itself may take anywhere from a few days to several weeks, depending on the number of test units required to adequately sample the site. Laboratory processing and analysis typically takes longer than the field phase, especially when radiocarbon dating, zooarchaeological analysis, or paleoethnobotanical studies are part of the scope.

SHPO review adds additional time after report submission. Most SHPOs request a 30-day review period, though complex or contested eligibility determinations may require additional consultation. Project managers should incorporate these review windows into master construction schedules to avoid delays. Early coordination with historic preservation professionals helps anticipate scope and timeline before fieldwork begins.

What Happens After a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

Once the Phase II report is complete and reviewed, the lead federal agency issues a formal eligibility determination in consultation with the SHPO and other consulting parties. This determination drives the remainder of the Section 106 process. Sites found not eligible are typically cleared, and the project may proceed in those areas without further archaeological mitigation.

Sites determined eligible for the National Register trigger a finding of adverse effect if the project will damage or destroy them. That finding initiates consultation to resolve the adverse effect — most commonly through Phase III data recovery excavation, though alternatives such as project redesign, avoidance, or preservation in place may be negotiated depending on site characteristics and project constraints.

The eligibility determination and any Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or Programmatic Agreement (PA) that results become part of the project's permanent compliance record. For developers and agencies, this documentation demonstrates due diligence under federal law and protects against future legal challenges. Experienced CRM research teams help ensure that reports meet agency standards the first time, avoiding costly revisions and delays.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between a Phase I and Phase II archaeological survey?

A Phase I survey identifies and documents archaeological sites within a project area through shovel testing and surface inspection. A Phase II evaluation focuses specifically on sites found during Phase I to determine whether they meet National Register of Historic Places eligibility criteria through controlled excavation and detailed analysis.

Who reviews and approves Phase II archaeological evaluation reports?

The lead federal agency and the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) review Phase II reports and jointly make eligibility determinations. Tribal Historic Preservation Offices (THPOs) and other consulting parties may also participate when sites have potential Native American cultural affiliation or other interests at stake.

Does every project require a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

No. A Phase II evaluation is only required when a Phase I survey identifies sites that cannot be assessed as eligible or not eligible without additional data. Many Phase I surveys result in a finding of no sites requiring further evaluation, and the federal undertaking can proceed without a Phase II investigation.

How much does a Phase II archaeological evaluation cost?

Costs vary widely based on site size, deposit complexity, laboratory requirements, and reporting standards. Small, straightforward evaluations may cost $15,000–$40,000, while complex multicomponent sites with extensive laboratory work can exceed $100,000. GIS and remote sensing tools can improve sampling efficiency and help manage costs.

Put Phase II Expertise to Work for Your Project

A well-executed Phase II archaeological evaluation protects your project by delivering defensible eligibility determinations that hold up to SHPO and agency scrutiny. Cutting corners at this stage risks delays, legal exposure, and costly Phase III mitigation that could have been avoided with thorough upfront work.

excerpt: Learn what a Phase II archaeological evaluation involves, when it's required under Section 106, and how eligibility determinations affect your federal project timeline.
